Cumulative Distribution Function of the Difference Between Two Beta-Binomial Proportions

Source: R/pbetabinomdiff.R
pbetabinomdiff.Rd

Calculates the cumulative distribution function (CDF) of the difference between two independent Beta-Binomial proportions by exact enumeration. Specifically, computes \(P((Y_1/m_1) - (Y_2/m_2) \le q)\) or \(P((Y_1/m_1) - (Y_2/m_2) > q)\), where \(Y_1 \sim \mathrm{BetaBinomial}(m_1, \alpha_1, \beta_1)\) and \(Y_2 \sim \mathrm{BetaBinomial}(m_2, \alpha_2, \beta_2)\).

Usage

pbetabinomdiff(q, m1, m2, alpha1, alpha2, beta1, beta2, lower.tail = TRUE)

Arguments

q

A numeric scalar representing the quantile threshold for the difference in proportions.

m1

A positive integer giving the number of trials for the first Beta-Binomial distribution.

m2

A positive integer giving the number of trials for the second Beta-Binomial distribution.

alpha1

A positive numeric scalar giving the first shape parameter of the first Beta mixing distribution.

alpha2

A positive numeric scalar giving the first shape parameter of the second Beta mixing distribution.

beta1

A positive numeric scalar giving the second shape parameter of the first Beta mixing distribution.

beta2

A positive numeric scalar giving the second shape parameter of the second Beta mixing distribution.

lower.tail

A logical scalar; if TRUE (default), the function returns \(P((Y_1/m_1) - (Y_2/m_2) \le q)\), otherwise \(P((Y_1/m_1) - (Y_2/m_2) > q)\).

Value

A numeric scalar in [0, 1].

Details

The probability mass function of \(Y \sim \mathrm{BetaBinomial}(m, \alpha, \beta)\) is: $$P(Y = k) = \binom{m}{k} \frac{B(k + \alpha,\; m - k + \beta)}{B(\alpha, \beta)}, \quad k = 0, \ldots, m$$ where \(B(\cdot, \cdot)\) is the Beta function.

The exact CDF is obtained by enumerating all \((m_1 + 1)(m_2 + 1)\) outcome combinations and summing the joint probabilities for which the proportion difference satisfies the specified condition. Computation time therefore grows quadratically in \(m_1\) and \(m_2\); for large trial sizes consider a normal approximation.

The Beta-Binomial distribution arises when the success probability in a Binomial model follows a Beta prior, making it appropriate for overdispersed count data and for posterior predictive calculations in Bayesian binary-endpoint trials.

Examples

# P((Y1/12) - (Y2/12) > 0.2) with symmetric Beta(0.5, 0.5) priors
pbetabinomdiff(0.2, 12, 12, 0.5, 0.5, 0.5, 0.5, lower.tail = FALSE)
#> [1] 0.3372581

# P((Y1/20) - (Y2/15) > 0.1) with different sample sizes
pbetabinomdiff(0.1, 20, 15, 1, 1, 1, 1, lower.tail = FALSE)
#> [1] 0.4017857

# P((Y1/10) - (Y2/10) > 0) with informative priors
pbetabinomdiff(0, 10, 10, 2, 3, 3, 2, lower.tail = FALSE)
#> [1] 0.2383331

# Lower tail: P((Y1/15) - (Y2/15) <= 0.05)
pbetabinomdiff(0.05, 15, 15, 1, 1, 1, 1, lower.tail = TRUE)
#> [1] 0.53125
